g.data-narration: Welcome to this module on Central Limit Theorem and Confidence Limits, essential tools for statistical inference in data science.
g.text: For Unknown Mean - A Complete Guide

g.data-narration: Point estimates give us a single number, but how confident can we be? Statistical inference quantifies this uncertainty.
g.text: In the previous module, you learned about point estimation - getting a single value (like sample mean x̄) to estimate population mean µ.
But how confident can we be in this estimate? Statistical inference provides tools to quantify this uncertainty.

g.data-narration: In this module, you will learn the Central Limit Theorem, how to construct confidence intervals, and work through a complete example.
The Central Limit Theorem states that sample means form a normal distribution regardless of the population's original shape.
g.text: "If you take sufficiently large random samples from ANY population with mean µ and finite variance σ²,"
"the distribution of sample means (x̄) will be approximately normally distributed."

g.data-narration: For CLT to apply, sample size must be sufficiently large. The rule of thumb is n ≥ 30 for most distributions.
g.text: Rule of thumb for most distributions
For highly skewed distributions, larger n may be needed

g.data-narration: The standard error measures variability of sample means. It decreases as sample size increases, making estimates more precise.
g.text: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is called the
population std dev / square root of sample size

g.data-narration: The mean of all sample means equals the population mean. This is why sample means are unbiased estimators.
g.text: The sampling distribution is centered at the true population mean

g.data-narration: For computer scientists, CLT enables normal-based inference on non-normal data like app load times and API response latencies.
g.text: Why CLT Matters for Computer Science
When analyzing metrics like app load times, API response latencies, or user engagement scores
CLT guarantees means will be normal!

g.data-narration: A confidence interval provides a range likely to contain the true population mean with specified confidence.
g.text: A point estimate gives ONE number. A confidence interval provides a RANGE of values
likely to contain the true population mean with a specified level of confidence.

g.data-narration: The 95% confidence interval formula is: x̄ ± Z × (σ/√n), where Z=1.96 for 95% confidence.
The correct interpretation: if we took many samples, 95% of their confidence intervals would contain the true population mean.
g.text: ✗ WRONG: "There is 95% probability the mean lies in this interval."
✓ CORRECT: "If we took many samples, 95% of the intervals would contain µ."

g.data-narration: When population sigma is unknown (common in real-world), use t-distribution with sample standard deviation s instead of Z.
g.text: What if σ is Unknown? (The Common Real-World Case)
In reality, we almost NEVER know the population standard deviation σ.
We use the t-distribution instead, which has heavier tails accounting for uncertainty in estimating σ.

g.data-narration: The t-distribution uses n-1 degrees of freedom. As sample size increases, it converges to the normal distribution.
Let's work through a complete example: n=40, x̄=152ms, s=28ms. We calculate SE, margin of error, and build the 95% CI.
g.text: Given: n = 40 queries, x̄ = 152 ms, s = 28 ms
Find: 95% confidence interval for true mean response time

g.data-narration: CLT enables normal-based inference. SE = σ/√n measures precision. Larger n means narrower, more precise confidence intervals.
Use Z-distribution when sigma is known. Use t-distribution when sigma is estimated from sample - this is the common real-world case.
g.text: When to Use Z vs t-Distribution
